Journal.Title: The Korean journal of gastroenterology = Taehan Sohwagi Hakhoe chi

Article.ArticleTitle: Downregulation of the Tumor Suppressor P53 Gene associated with the Progression of Clinical Staging and the Incidence of Distant Metastasis in Indonesian Colorectal Cancer.

Abstract.AbstractText: A genome-wide study identified tumor suppressor P53 (TP53), BRAF, KRAS, COL-3A1, and SOCS-2 as key drivers of tumorigenesis in human colorectal cancers (CRC). We investigated the association between these molecules' expression levels and the progression of clinical stage as well as the occurrence of distant metastasis in CRC.

We recruited adult patients who underwent colonoscopy and had a histologically confirmed diagnosis of CRC. Clinical staging was determined following extensive workups. Immunohistochemistry (IHC) was used to evaluate the expression level of TP53, KRAS, BRAF, COL-3A1 and SOCS-2 in tumor biopsies.

The study involved 63 CRC patients, with a distribution across different stages: 1 (1.6%) in stage I, 6 (9.5%) in stage II, 30 (47.6%) in stage III, and 26 (41.3%) in stage IV. The expression level of TP53 gene were inversely correlated with clinical stages (ρ -0.260, p<0.05). Patients with distant metastases had a significantly lower expression of TP53 compared to those without (0.00 [1.00] vs. 1.00 [23.00], p<0.05). Subanalysis of patients with left-sided tumors demonstrates a significantly reduced expression level of TP53 in both lung (0.00 [0.00] vs. 1.00 [5.25], p<0.05) and overall (0.00 [1.00] vs. 1.00 [21.50], p<0.05) metastases. The expression of TP53 was also positively correlated with BRAF, KRAS, COL-3A1, and SOCS-2 (ρ -0.617, p<0.05; ρ -0.272, p<0.05; ρ 0.348, p<0.05; ρ 0.571, p<0.05).
TP53 is downregulated in advanced clinical stages and distant metastases, demonstrating its role in aggressive nature of CRC.

Article.ArticleTitle: The crosstalk between autophagy and ferroptosis in pulmonary fibrosis.

Abstract.AbstractText: Pulmonary fibrosis (PF) is a progressive lung disorder characterized by excessive deposition of extracellular matrix (ECM) in the alveoli, with irreversible fibrotic remodeling and destruction of alveolar structures. Clinically, PF is manifested by progressive dyspnea and a decline in lung function. These manifestations contribute to a poor prognosis and significantly diminish the quality of life of affected individuals. Current therapeutic options for PF remain limited, highlighting the urgent need to elucidate its molecular mechanisms in order to develop novel treatment strategies. Recent studies have revealed that autophagy and ferroptosis, two critical cell death pathways, engage in intricate crosstalk mechanisms that regulate the pathogenesis of PF. Autophagy maintains cellular homeostasis by mediating lysosome-dependent degradation, whereas ferroptosis is characterized by iron-dependent lipid peroxidation. The interplay between autophagy and ferroptosis plays a pivotal role in modulating fibrosis progression. However, the mechanistic interactions between autophagy and ferroptosis in PF remain poorly understood, particularly regarding their bidirectional crosstalk and their unique role in PF progression. This review aims to systematically synthesize the current understanding of autophagy-ferroptosis interactions in PF, thereby identifying potential therapeutic strategies and drug targets for PF treatment.

Journal.Title: The Science of the total environment

Article.ArticleTitle: Exploring the mechanism of volatile organic compounds in respiratory tract tumors based on network toxicology and molecular simulation.

Abstract.AbstractText: This study elucidates the pathogenic role of environmental volatile organic compounds (VOCs) in respiratory tumorigenesis, focusing on head and neck cancer (HNSC), lung squamous cell carcinoma (LUSC), and lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD). As pervasive pollutants derived from industrial emissions, traffic exhaust, and indoor contaminants, VOCs threaten public health, yet their molecular mechanisms in multi-cancer pathogenesis remain poorly defined. By integrating network toxicology and molecular docking, we systematically dissected VOC-driven disruptions in respiratory homeostasis. Disease-associated targets were mined from TCGA, CTD, and GeneCards databases, followed by core target identification via STRING network analysis and Cytoscape. Functional enrichment (GO/KEGG) and survival analyses revealed critical pathways, while molecular docking and molecular dynamics validated pollutant-target interactions. Our results demonstrate that VOCs directly perturb key targets (FN1, PECAM1, CDK1 etc.), hijacking PI3K-Akt signaling, ECM-receptor interactions, and cell cycle regulation to promote apoptosis evasion and unchecked proliferation. High-affinity binding between VOCs and targets underscores their role as environmental carcinogens. Strikingly, shared dysregulated pathway across HNSC, LUSC, and LUAD highlight pan-respiratory susceptibility to VOC exposure. Epidemiological correlations align with molecular findings, emphasizing cumulative risks in populations exposed to air pollution. The study also highlights FN1 and PECAM1 as key targets, emphasizing the importance of these markers in VOC-associated carcinogenesis.
